What's the difference between ASCAP, BMI, and SESAC? Which one should I join? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"pop"}}]

A PRO (Performing Rights Organization) collects performance royalties when your music is played publicly — radio, streaming, TV, live venues, restaurants, etc. You MUST join one.

**PRO Comparison:**

| Feature | ASCAP | BMI | SESAC |
|---------|-------|-----|-------|
| Membership cost | $50 (one-time) | Free | Invitation only |
| Payment frequency | Quarterly | Quarterly | Monthly |
| Payment speed | 6-9 months after performance | 5-7 months | 3-4 months |
| Notable members | Beyoncé, Stevie Wonder | Taylor Swift, Eminem | Adele, Bob Dylan |
| Online tools | Good portal | Good portal | Excellent portal |
| Registration ease | Easy, online | Easy, online | Must be accepted |
| Advances | For established members | For established members | Available earlier |

**My Recommendation:**

*For most independent artists:*
→ **BMI** — Free to join, no upfront cost, good digital tracking

*If you have startup budget:*
→ **ASCAP** — $50 one-time, slightly faster payment processing recently

*If you're established:*
→ **SESAC** — Invitation only, but fastest payments and proactive about placements

**Critical: Register as BOTH Writer AND Publisher**
1. Join as a songwriter (writer share = 50% of performance royalties)
2. Create a publishing entity and register as a publisher (publisher share = 50%)
3. If you don't register as your own publisher, the publisher share sits UNCOLLECTED
4. This is literally leaving 50% of your performance royalties on the table

**Registration Checklist:**
- [ ] Join PRO as songwriter
- [ ] Create publishing entity (LLC or DBA — just a name is fine to start)
- [ ] Register with same PRO as publisher
- [ ] Register ALL existing songs
- [ ] Get your IPI/CAE number (assigned upon registration)

I just released a new single. I know I need to register it for royalties somewhere but I'm not sure what to do. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"r&b"}}]

You're ahead of 90% of independent artists just by asking this question. Most people release and leave money on the table for years. Let me walk you through the three registrations you need.

Running `register_work` to start the process.

**Registration 1 — Your PRO (ASCAP or BMI):**
This collects your PERFORMANCE royalties — money earned when your song is played publicly (streaming, radio, live venues, TV).
- Log into your PRO account → "Register a Work"
- Enter: song title, writers (your name + any co-writers), percentage splits, ISRC code, release date
- If you registered as your own publisher (you should have), register the song under your publisher entity too

**Registration 2 — The MLC (themlc.com):**
This collects your MECHANICAL royalties from interactive streaming (Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon Music).
- This is a SEPARATE royalty from what your distributor pays you. Your distributor pays you the master recording royalty. The MLC pays you the composition mechanical.
- Log into themlc.com → Register your song with the same metadata
- If you don't register here, this money sits in a black box

**Registration 3 — SoundExchange:**
This collects your NEIGHBORING RIGHTS / digital performance royalties from non-interactive platforms (Pandora, SiriusXM, internet radio).
- Log into SoundExchange → Register as both Artist AND Sound Recording Copyright Owner

**The money flow after all three registrations:**
- Spotify stream → Your distributor pays you the master royalty
- Spotify stream → MLC pays you the mechanical royalty (composition)
- Spotify stream → Your PRO pays you the performance royalty (composition)
- Pandora play → SoundExchange pays you the neighboring rights royalty

One stream, four potential royalty sources. Without all three registrations, you're only collecting ONE of them through your distributor.

My ASCAP statements seem really low for the number of streams I'm getting. How do I know if I'm being paid correctly? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

PRO royalty discrepancies are extremely common — and most go unnoticed because artists don't know how to audit. Here's your detective playbook:

Common reasons ASCAP payments are too low: (1) Incomplete work registration — your song isn't properly registered with ASCAP, so royalties generated by it go to the 'unmatched' pool. Check: log into ASCAP's ACE database (ascap.com/repertory) and search for each of your song titles. Verify: correct title, all writers listed, correct percentages, your IPI number attached. (2) Missing or incorrect ISRC/ISWC links — ASCAP needs these identifiers to match streaming data to your songs. If the ISRC on your distribution doesn't match what ASCAP has, royalties may go uncollected. (3) Co-writer registration issues — if your co-writer is with BMI but didn't register their share, the full song may be partially unmatched. Both writers' PROs need the work registered. (4) International performances not flowing back — ASCAP has reciprocal agreements with international PROs, but these can take 12-24 months to process.

How to audit: (1) Download your ASCAP statement detail (CSV/PDF). (2) Cross-reference with your Spotify for Artists data — identify tracks with significant streams that DON'T appear on your ASCAP statement. (3) For each missing track, verify registration in the ASCAP repertory. (4) Contact ASCAP Member Services with specific examples: 'Track [title], ISRC [code], had X streams in Q1 2026 per Spotify for Artists but does not appear on my statement dated [date].'

Expected pay rate from ASCAP for streaming: ASCAP's per-stream performance royalty rate varies but is typically $0.002-$0.004 per stream for the songwriter's share. If your track had 500,000 streams and your ASCAP payment for that track is under $300, something may be wrong.

Preventive measures: (1) Register every song with your PRO within 2 weeks of release. (2) Include ISRC codes in the registration. (3) Verify co-writers have registered their shares with their respective PROs. (4) Check the ACE/BMI repertory databases quarterly to ensure your catalog is properly reflected. (5) Consider using Songtrust or TuneCore Publishing to handle registrations globally.

ASCAP pays quarterly. There's an inherent 6-9 month delay between when a stream occurs and when you receive the royalty payment. Factor this into your analysis.

I keep getting confused between a 'publisher,' an 'admin,' and a PRO. Can you explain what each one does and which ones I actually need? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

These three entities do different things, and you likely need at least two of them. Here's the clearest breakdown possible:

PRO (Performance Rights Organization) — ASCAP, BMI, SESAC, GMR: What they do: collect PERFORMANCE royalties when your song is played publicly — radio, streaming, live venues, businesses, TV. Who pays them: radio stations, venues, streaming platforms, and businesses pay blanket license fees to PROs. Your role: register as a songwriter member. Register every song you write. Cost: ASCAP: $50 one-time. BMI: free for writers. You NEED one: yes, absolutely. This is non-negotiable for any songwriter.

Publishing Administrator — Songtrust, TuneCore Publishing, CD Baby Pro: What they do: register your songs with COLLECTION SOCIETIES worldwide, collect royalties you'd otherwise miss (international performance royalties, mechanical royalties from The MLC), and handle licensing administration. What they DON'T do: actively pitch your music for sync, provide creative services, or advance you money. Fee: typically 10-20% of collected royalties + a small annual fee. You NEED one if: you want to collect international royalties efficiently, you want someone to handle MLC registration and global society registration, you don't have a publishing deal.

Publisher (Traditional) — Sony/ATV, Warner Chappell, Universal Music Publishing, or indie publishers: What they do: everything an admin does PLUS: actively pitch your songs for sync placements, connect you with co-writers and opportunities, provide creative direction and A&R, advance you money against future royalties, and exploit your catalog for maximum revenue. What they take: typically 25-50% of your publishing income (co-pub or full pub deal). You NEED one if: you want active sync pitching and creative services, you need an advance, your catalog is earning enough to attract publisher interest (usually $20,000+/year in publishing income).

The stack for most indie artists: Step 1 (essential): join ASCAP or BMI as a writer. Step 2 (essential): register with The MLC (free). Step 3 (essential): register with SoundExchange (free). Step 4 (recommended): create your own publishing entity and affiliate with your PRO. Step 5 (recommended): sign up for a publishing admin (Songtrust, $100/year) for global collection. Step 6 (optional/later): if your publishing income grows to $15,000+/year, consider approaching a traditional publisher for a co-pub deal.

Common mistakes: Thinking your PRO handles everything (they only handle performance royalties). Thinking your distributor handles publishing (they handle master recording royalties — different thing). Not registering with The MLC (mechanical royalties go uncollected). Not registering with SoundExchange (digital performance royalties go uncollected). Signing a publishing deal too early when an admin service would suffice.

A publishing company reached out and wants to sign me. Should I do it? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging"}}]

If your goal is to stay independent, the short answer is: you don't need to sign with a publisher to collect your publishing income. Here's what you can do yourself right now at zero cost:

1. **Join a PRO** — ASCAP or BMI (both free to join; ASCAP has a one-time $50 writer registration). They collect performance royalties every time your music is streamed, broadcast, or performed publicly.
2. **Register with the MLC** (Mechanical Licensing Collective) — Free. They collect mechanical royalties from streaming services under the Music Modernization Act. If you're not registered, that money sits in a pool and eventually gets distributed to other writers.
3. **Set up a publishing admin account** — Services like Songtrust ($75 one-time), DistroKid Publishing, or CD Baby Pro register your songs internationally and collect from sub-publishers in countries where you'd otherwise miss royalties. Cost is minimal, no ownership given up.

That setup collects the same royalties a traditional publisher would collect — and you keep 100% of your publishing rights.

Where a publisher deal makes sense: if they're offering a meaningful cash advance you need for recording, they have specific sync relationships that will get your music placed, or they can open co-writing sessions with established artists you couldn't access otherwise. But those are concrete, specific benefits — not a vague promise of 'development.'

Bottom line: self-publish first. Build leverage. Sign a publishing deal only when you know exactly what you're getting in exchange for the ownership you're giving up.

I keep hearing about blockchain for music royalties and NFTs for publishing. Is any of this real or just hype? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Kai","genre":"Electronic","monthlyListeners":80000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Kai, the intersection of blockchain and music publishing in 2026 is a mix of genuine innovation and residual hype. Let me separate fact from fiction.

What Is Real and Working:

1. Royalty Tokenization: Platforms like Royal.io and Bolero allow songwriters to sell fractional ownership of their publishing royalties to fans and investors. You tokenize a percentage of your royalty stream (e.g., sell 10% of your song's publishing royalties for $5,000 upfront). This is a legitimate fundraising mechanism used by real artists.

2. Blockchain-Based Rights Management: Companies like Revelator and Original Works are building blockchain-based registries for composition rights. The promise: instant, transparent tracking of who owns what across the global publishing chain. Status: still emerging, not yet replacing traditional PRO/MLC infrastructure.

3. Smart Contract Splits: Automated royalty splitting using smart contracts. When revenue arrives, it is automatically distributed per the agreed percentages with no human processing delay. Some artists use this for direct-to-fan sales.

What Is Still Hype or Early:

1. Replacing PROs with Blockchain: Not happening yet. PROs handle billions in complex, regulation-governed transactions. Blockchain cannot yet match this scale or the legal framework around it.

2. NFT Albums as Publishing: Simply selling an NFT of your music does NOT automatically convey publishing rights. An NFT is a token of ownership of a digital asset — it does NOT inherently transfer copyright unless explicitly structured to do so via a legal agreement.

3. Decentralized Publishing: The vision of fully decentralized music publishing (no intermediaries) remains theoretical. Collection societies exist because of legal mandates, not just inefficiency.

Practical Advice for You:
- Royalty tokenization can be a legitimate way to raise capital without giving up publishing long-term (if the deal is structured with a time limit)
- Do NOT sell perpetual royalty interests via NFTs without legal counsel
- Any blockchain-based rights system you use should be IN ADDITION to, not instead of, your PRO and MLC registrations
- The technology is promising but not yet a replacement for traditional publishing infrastructure

What are the current CRB mechanical royalty rates for interactive streaming in 2026? I need the actual numbers for a publishing deal negotiation. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"country"}}]

The CRB (Copyright Royalty Board) set rates for interactive streaming under Phonorecords IV, covering 2023-2027. Here are the operative 2026 numbers:

**Interactive streaming mechanical rates (2026):**
- Greater of: (a) **$0.00229 per stream** OR (b) **26.2% of service revenue** minus performance royalties paid, OR (c) **26.2% of total content costs** — whichever yields the highest payment to rights holders
- For paid subscription services (Spotify Premium tier): the rate is calculated on the per-subscriber minimum
- For free/ad-supported tier: lower effective rates apply (~$0.00080-0.00120/stream effective)

**What this means practically:**
The statutory mechanical rate you're owed per stream from interactive on-demand services is approximately $0.00229. The challenge: most DSPs calculate against the revenue formula (26.2%), which in practice yields more than the per-stream floor for larger services. The mechanical portion is collected by the MLC and distributed to publishers and songwriters.

**For your negotiation:**
If a publisher proposes paying you less than the statutory mechanical on your owned compositions, they're proposing a below-market deal. Statutory rates are the floor, not the ceiling — direct licensing deals can exceed them. In a co-pub or admin deal, confirm that 100% of statutory mechanicals flow to you (your writer's share + your publisher's share) minus only their administration fee, which should be 10-25% of net receipts.

Note: Always verify current rates at copyright.gov/licensing-center — these can adjust annually.

I recorded a cover of a popular song and want to release it on Spotify. Do I need permission? How does the money work? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Great question — covers are one of the most misunderstood areas of music licensing. You DO need a license, but the good news is you have a legal right to obtain one.

The law: Under U.S. copyright law (Section 115), once a song has been commercially released, anyone can record and release their own version by obtaining a compulsory mechanical license. The original artist/songwriter cannot refuse — it's compulsory. BUT you must: not change the fundamental character of the composition (no parodies or major lyric changes without permission), pay the statutory mechanical royalty rate, and obtain the license BEFORE releasing.

The 2024-2026 statutory rate: $0.12 per copy/download (for songs under 5 minutes). For streaming, it's a percentage of the DSP's revenue allocated per play (the rate is set by the Copyright Royalty Board).

How to get the license: Option 1 — Through your distributor: DistroKid offers cover song licensing for $12/year per track. They handle the mechanical license and pay the royalties automatically. This is the easiest option. Option 2 — Harry Fox Agency (HFA): The traditional way. Apply at harryfox.com for a mechanical license. Cost: ~$16 per song + the per-unit royalty. Option 3 — Easy Song Licensing, Songfile, or Loudr: Third-party services that handle the paperwork.

How the money works: You keep the master recording royalties (you made the recording). You pay mechanical royalties to the original songwriter/publisher (via your license). You do NOT earn publishing/composition royalties — those belong to the original songwriter. Your distributor's cut is taken from your master royalty.

Real example: You release a cover on Spotify via DistroKid. Your cover gets 100,000 streams. Your master royalty at $0.004/stream: $400. DistroKid's cover licensing fee: $12/year. Mechanical royalty paid to original songwriter: handled by DistroKid from the streaming pool. Your net: ~$388.

What you CANNOT do: Release a cover without a mechanical license (illegal). Substantially change the lyrics or composition without separate permission. Record a cover of a song that has never been commercially released (compulsory license doesn't apply). Use the original artist's recording (that's sampling, not covering — completely different license).

What's the difference between my master and my publishing? Everyone keeps saying these words and I don't really understand them. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

This is the single most important concept in the music business, so let's nail it.

You have TWO separate copyrights in every song you record:

**1. The Master Recording** — the actual audio file, the specific recording. Whoever owns the master gets paid every time that recording is streamed, downloaded, or licensed. If you recorded it yourself and paid for the session, YOU own the master — full stop.

**2. The Publishing (Composition)** — the underlying song: the melody, the lyrics, the chords. This exists separately from any specific recording. Whoever owns the publishing gets paid mechanical royalties (when the song is reproduced — streams, downloads, CDs) and performance royalties (when the song is performed or played publicly — radio, TV, live).

Why this matters for you right now:
- If you write and record your own music, you own BOTH — master AND publishing. That means two separate royalty streams.
- If a label signs you and takes ownership of your masters, they own the recording. You may still own your publishing — but they'll often try to take a cut of that too (that's a publishing deal or a 360 deal).
- Your first move: register your songs with a PRO (ASCAP or BMI) to collect performance royalties on the publishing side. It's free and you're leaving real money on the table every day you're not registered.

The goal as an indie artist: own both, forever, and get paid from both streams.

I own my masters and I've been releasing music for 4 years. Someone told me I'm owed neighboring rights royalties from Europe that I've never collected. How does this work? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"electronic"}}]

You are almost certainly owed money you haven't collected. This is one of the most significant uncollected revenue streams for independent artists who own their masters.

**What neighboring rights are:**
Neighboring rights (also called 'related rights' or 'ancillary rights') are royalties paid to **performing artists and master rights owners** when recorded music is played in public — terrestrial radio, satellite radio, TV broadcasts, restaurants, clubs. This is SEPARATE from the songwriter's performance royalties (ASCAP/BMI). As a master owner who also performs, you're entitled to both.

**In the US:** SoundExchange collects neighboring rights from digital broadcasters (Pandora, SiriusXM, iHeart digital). Register free at soundexchange.com. They also have a retroactive claims window — you can recover up to 3 years of uncollected royalties after registering.

**In the UK:** PPL (Phonographic Performance Ltd) collects from broadcasting and public performance. Register at ppluk.com — they cover UK and international reciprocal territories.

**Internationally:** SCAPR (Societies of Performers) member organizations collect in 45+ countries. Key societies: GVL (Germany), IFPI Danmark, RAAP (Ireland), etc. Many have reciprocal agreements, meaning if you register with PPL or SoundExchange, they'll collect and forward from member societies.

**4 years unregistered:** Retroactive recovery varies by society — typically 2-3 years back. Some unclaimed royalties revert to the society after a statutory period. Register with SoundExchange and PPL this week. Consider working with a neighboring rights agency (Sentric, Recorded Artist Agency) who collect across all territories for a 15-25% commission — worth it given the complexity.

I have fans in Japan, Germany, and Brazil but I'm only registered with ASCAP in the US. Am I losing money from international streams? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Yes — you are almost certainly leaving international royalties uncollected. Here's how global royalty collection works:

How international royalties flow: When your song is streamed in Japan, the Japanese PRO (JASRAC) collects the performance royalties. JASRAC has a reciprocal agreement with ASCAP. In theory, JASRAC sends your royalties to ASCAP, and ASCAP pays you. In reality: this process is slow (12-24+ months), collection rates are imperfect (ASCAP may only collect 60-80% of what's owed from certain territories), and some smaller territories have weak reciprocal agreements or none at all.

Major international PROs and territories: GEMA (Germany/Austria). SACEM (France). PRS for Music (UK). JASRAC (Japan). ABRAMUS/ECAD (Brazil). SOCAN (Canada). APRA AMCOS (Australia/New Zealand). SIAE (Italy). SGAE (Spain).

The problem for indie artists: ASCAP's reciprocal agreements cover major territories but the collection rate and speed varies. Micro-payments from smaller territories may fall below threshold amounts and never get distributed. Mechanical royalties (different from performance royalties) have their own international collection challenges.

Solutions: Option 1 — Publishing Administrator with Global Reach: Services like Songtrust ($100/year), TuneCore Publishing, or CD Baby Pro register your songs directly with international collection societies in 60+ countries. This significantly increases your international collection rate (often 20-40% more than relying on reciprocal agreements alone). They take 10-15% commission but the uplift in collected royalties more than covers it.

Option 2 — Sub-Publishing Deals: For more established catalogs, a sub-publisher in a specific territory will actively administer your catalog there. Example: having a sub-publisher in Japan who registers with JASRAC directly and actively monitors your royalties. More hands-on, higher collection rate, but typically requires a minimum earning threshold.

Option 3 — Multiple PRO Registrations: Some artists register with international PROs directly. This is complex and can create conflicts with your primary ASCAP membership. Not recommended unless you have professional guidance.

Recommendation for your situation: Sign up for Songtrust or TuneCore Publishing ($50-$100/year). They will register your songs with international societies directly, bypassing the slower reciprocal process. Expected impact: 20-40% increase in international royalty collection, especially from territories like Japan, Germany, and Brazil where you have demonstrated listener activity.

What is SoundExchange? My friend said I'm probably owed money I don't know about. [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Your friend is probably right — SoundExchange holds unclaimed royalties for a huge number of independent artists. Here's what it is and why you need it:

What SoundExchange does: SoundExchange collects digital performance royalties for the use of sound recordings on NON-INTERACTIVE platforms. Non-interactive means the listener doesn't choose what plays — think: Pandora (internet radio), SiriusXM (satellite radio), iHeartRadio, cable music channels, and webcasters. This is DIFFERENT from interactive streaming (Spotify, Apple Music — where you choose what to play).

Why it's separate: The royalties SoundExchange collects are mandated by federal law (Section 114 of the Copyright Act). These are owed to both the copyright owner (label/artist who owns the master) AND the performing artist. SoundExchange splits them: 50% to the copyright owner (you, if you're independent and own your masters). 45% to the featured artist. 5% to the AFM/SAG-AFTRA fund (for session musicians and backup vocalists).

As a self-released indie artist who owns your masters: you receive both the copyright owner share (50%) AND the featured artist share (45%) = 95% total. This is unique — it's one of the few royalty types where the performer gets paid SEPARATELY from the copyright owner.

How much money: A song played 1,000 times on Pandora might generate $5-$8 in SoundExchange royalties. That sounds small, but it compounds across your catalog and over time. SoundExchange has paid out over $10 billion since its founding. They're holding over $250 million in unclaimed royalties.

How to register (FREE): Go to soundexchange.com. Register as BOTH a 'Sound Recording Copyright Owner' (SRCO) AND a 'Featured Artist.' Provide your legal name, a government-issued ID, and your catalog information (ISRCs help). There is no membership fee — SoundExchange takes a standard admin fee (currently ~5-6%) from royalties collected.

Common confusions: SoundExchange is NOT a PRO (not ASCAP/BMI). SoundExchange pays royalties on RECORDINGS. PROs pay royalties on COMPOSITIONS. These are separate income streams for the same play. Your distributor does NOT collect SoundExchange royalties (with some exceptions — check with your distributor). SoundExchange does NOT collect interactive streaming royalties (that's your distributor). Many artists confuse SoundExchange with The MLC — they are completely different organizations collecting different types of royalties.

I co-wrote a song with two other people. How do we split the royalties fairly? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"R&B"}}]

The #1 rule in publishing: **AGREE ON SPLITS BEFORE YOU LEAVE THE ROOM.** This avoids 90% of music industry disputes.

**The Split Sheet (What It Is):**
A written agreement documenting each contributor's ownership percentage of a song's composition. It's a legally binding document.

**What to Include:**
- Song title and working title (if different)
- Date of creation
- Names of ALL contributors
- Percentage ownership for each contributor
- PRO affiliation for each contributor (ASCAP, BMI, SESAC)
- IPI/CAE number for each contributor
- Publisher information (if any contributor has a publishing deal)
- Signatures from ALL parties

**Common Split Approaches:**

| Method | How It Works | When to Use |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| Equal split | 33.3% / 33.3% / 33.3% | All contributed equally, simplest |
| Role-based | Lyrics writer gets X%, melody gets Y%, production gets Z% | Clear role separation |
| Contribution-based | Negotiated per contribution level | Complex sessions, varying input |

**Best Practices:**
1. **Sign BEFORE releasing.** No exceptions.
2. **Include producers.** If the producer contributed to the composition (melody, chord changes, arrangement ideas), they deserve a publishing split. Production work alone (mixing, engineering) is NOT a publishing contribution.
3. **Samples count.** If you sampled someone, their split comes off the top before your team splits.
4. **Use a template.** Free split sheet templates from ASCAP, BMI, or Songtrust.
5. **Register independently.** Each co-writer registers the song with their own PRO, listing ALL writers and their splits.

**The Conversation Script:**
'Before we finish, let's get the splits down. I think [X] makes sense because [reasoning]. What do you think?'

Have this conversation while the creative energy is good — not months later when money is involved.

An artist in Brazil wants to translate my song lyrics into Portuguese and record their own version. What are the publishing implications? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Lara","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":120000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Lara, a lyric translation creates a 'derivative work' — a new version of your original composition. Here is how the publishing rights work.

What Is a Derivative Work?
A derivative work is a new creative work based on an existing copyrighted work. Translations, adaptations, arrangements, and dramatizations are all derivative works. The original copyright owner (you) must authorize the creation of any derivative work.

Publishing Implications of a Translation:

1. Your Permission Is Required: The Brazilian artist cannot translate and release without your authorization. As the copyright owner, you have the exclusive right to prepare derivative works.

2. The Translator May Earn Co-Writer Credit: If the translator creates new lyrical content (not just a literal word-for-word translation), they may be entitled to a share of the derivative work's publishing. Common splits: translator gets 10-25% of the new version's publishing, you retain 75-90%.

3. A Literal Translation: If it is a direct, faithful translation with minimal creative interpretation, the translator typically receives no publishing share — they are acting as a translator, not a songwriter.

4. Two Registrations Needed: the original version (registered with your PRO, 100% your publishing) and the Portuguese version (registered as a derivative work, with split per your agreement).

5. Mechanical License: The Brazilian artist needs a mechanical license to record and distribute the derivative version. Since you own the publishing, you can grant this directly.

How to Structure the Deal:
1. Written license agreement specifying: permission to translate, approved territories (Brazil only? All Portuguese-speaking territories?), revenue split, credit format, duration of the license, whether they can create further derivatives.

2. ISWC: The translated version should receive its own ISWC, distinct from the original.

3. PRO Registration: Both versions registered accurately, with the Portuguese version linked to your original as a derivative.

This could be a great opportunity to expand your audience in Brazil — Spotify's third-largest market globally.

**The complication — Sony's claim:**
Your co-writer is signed to Sony Music Publishing. That means Sony owns or administers their share of the publishing. If you agree on a 50/50 split:

- Your publishing entity name (or self-published designation)

I keep seeing 'Writer's Share' and 'Publisher's Share' mentioned. As an independent artist who is my own publisher, how does this work? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Rio","genre":"R&B","monthlyListeners":80000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Rio, this is one of the most important concepts in publishing. Let me break it down clearly.

The 200% Model:
In music publishing, royalties are divided into two halves, expressed as percentages of the WHOLE:
- Writer's Share: 50% (also called the 'songwriter share')
- Publisher's Share: 50% (also called the 'publishing share')

These always add up to 100%, but in the industry they are often expressed as being out of 200% to reflect the two separate halves.

What Each Share Means:

Writer's Share (50%):
- Belongs to the SONGWRITER
- Paid directly to the songwriter by the PRO
- CANNOT be assigned or given away — this is sacrosanct
- Even if you sign a publishing deal, your Writer's Share stays with you
- If there are 2 co-writers (50/50 split), each gets 25% of the total (half of the Writer's Share)

Publisher's Share (50%):
- Belongs to whoever PUBLISHES the song
- If you have NO publisher → this share goes uncollected unless you set up a publishing entity
- If you sign with a publisher → they collect this share (and keep a portion)
- If you self-publish → you collect both shares yourself

As a Self-Published Independent:
You need to set up a publishing entity to collect the Publisher's Share:
1. Create a publishing company name (e.g., 'Rio Music Publishing')
2. Register it as a publisher with your PRO (ASCAP, BMI, etc.)
3. Now your PRO pays you BOTH shares: the Writer's Share to your songwriter account and the Publisher's Share to your publisher account

Without this step, you are only collecting 50% of your publishing royalties through your songwriter PRO account. The other 50% (Publisher's Share) sits uncollected.

The Math at 80K Monthly Listeners:
If your total publishing royalty is $200/month, without publisher registration you only receive $100 (Writer's Share). With publisher registration you receive the full $200.

Common Publishing Deal Impact:
- Admin Deal: Publisher takes 10-20% of the Publisher's Share. You keep 80-90% of Publisher's Share + 100% of Writer's Share.
- Co-Publishing: Publisher takes 50% of the Publisher's Share (25% of total). You keep the other 50% of Publisher's Share + 100% of Writer's Share = 75% total.
- Full Publishing: Publisher takes 100% of the Publisher's Share. You keep only your Writer's Share = 50% total. Avoid this unless the advance is significant.

I interpolated a melody from a 1970s R&B song in my new track. The label that owns the original publishing wants 50% of my publishing. Is that fair? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Jay","genre":"R&B","monthlyListeners":140000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Jay, interpolation publishing splits are among the most hotly negotiated terms in music. Here is the reality.

Interpolation vs. Sampling:
- Sampling: Using the actual sound recording (requires BOTH master license and publishing clearance)
- Interpolation: Re-performing/re-recording elements of the original composition (requires only publishing clearance)

Since you interpolated (re-performed the melody), you only need to clear the PUBLISHING rights, not the master recording.

Is 50% Fair?
There is no standard rate — it depends on how much of the original you used and the negotiating power of each party. The spectrum:

- 10-15%: Minor interpolation — a short melodic phrase, a few notes, not the main hook
- 25-35%: Moderate use — a recognizable melody used in your chorus or verse
- 40-50%: Significant use — the interpolated melody IS the signature element of your song
- 60-85%: The original is the foundation — heard in extreme cases like Vanilla Ice / Mariah Carey 'Fantasy'

For a recognizable melody from a 1970s R&B classic, 25-40% is the most common negotiated range. 50% is on the higher end and IS negotiable.

Negotiation Strategies:
1. Argue the degree of use: If the interpolated melody is only in your chorus (not the entire song), argue for a lower percentage
2. Counter-offer: Start at 20-25% and negotiate up. The original publisher will have started at 50% expecting to come down
3. Consider the alternatives: Could you modify your melody enough to avoid clearance? Sometimes changing 2-3 notes removes the interpolation risk entirely
4. Backend vs. upfront: Offer a higher upfront payment in exchange for a lower percentage of ongoing royalties

Critical Points:
- Get the clearance agreement IN WRITING before releasing the song
- The split applies to the COMPOSITION only — your master recording ownership is unaffected
- Register the song with your PRO listing both the original writers AND yourself with the agreed percentages
- If you do NOT clear the interpolation and the original publisher discovers it, they can demand 100% of publishing retroactively (plus damages)

Do I really need to register my songs with the Copyright Office? I thought copyright is automatic when you create something. [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

You're correct that copyright IS automatic upon creation — your song is copyrighted the moment you fix it in a tangible form (write it down or record it). However, REGISTERING that copyright with the U.S. Copyright Office unlocks critical legal protections:

Why register: (1) Statutory damages: without registration, you can only sue for 'actual damages' (the money you can prove you lost). With registration, you can claim statutory damages of $750 to $150,000 PER INFRINGEMENT — even if you can't prove specific financial loss. For willful infringement: up to $150,000 per work. (2) Attorney's fees: registration allows a court to award attorney's fees to the winning party. Without registration, you pay your own legal costs even if you win. (3) Presumption of validity: if registered within 5 years of publication, your registration is prima facie evidence of your copyright. The infringer has to prove you DON'T own it, rather than you having to prove you DO. (4) Required for federal lawsuits: you cannot file a federal copyright infringement lawsuit without registration (or a pending application).

What to register: (1) Sound recording (Form SR): protects the specific recorded performance and production. (2) Musical work (Form PA): protects the composition — the melody and lyrics, independent of any specific recording. Ideally, register BOTH for each release.

How to register: (1) Go to copyright.gov → eCO (Electronic Copyright Office). (2) Create an account. (3) Choose your application type: single work: $65 (one song). Group registration of unpublished works: $85 (up to 10 songs in one application — BEST VALUE). Group registration of published works: $85 (must share the same publication date). (4) Upload your audio files (MP3 or WAV). (5) Provide claimant information, title, authors, and publication date. (6) Processing time: 3-7 months (standard). Expedited: $800 (useful if you need to file a lawsuit immediately).

Timing: Register BEFORE publication if possible. If not, register within 3 months of publication to preserve the right to statutory damages and attorney's fees. If you register AFTER infringement occurs (but before or within 3 months of publication), you can still claim statutory damages.

Practical approach: Batch register: accumulate 10 unpublished songs, register them as a group for $85. That's $8.50 per song for critical legal protection. Register before your release date.

The 'poor man's copyright' myth: Mailing yourself a copy of your song does NOT constitute registration and has NO legal standing. Don't waste a stamp.

Can you explain exactly how I get paid when someone streams my song? Like the actual path the money takes from Spotify to my bank account for publishing. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Sage","genre":"Indie Folk","monthlyListeners":60000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Sage, great question. There are actually MULTIPLE money flows happening from a single stream. Let me trace each one.

When Someone Streams Your Song on Spotify:

Flow 1 — Master Recording Royalty (Your Sound Recording):
Spotify → Your Distributor (DistroKid, TuneCore, etc.) → Your Bank Account
This is the recording royalty for your specific performance. Your distributor handles this entirely.

Flow 2 — Performance Royalty (Your Composition):
Spotify → ASCAP/BMI/SESAC (your PRO) → Your Bank Account
This is the public performance royalty for your underlying composition. Your PRO collects this from Spotify's performance royalty pool.

Flow 3 — Mechanical Royalty (Your Composition):
Spotify → The MLC (Mechanical Licensing Collective) → Your Bank Account
This is the reproduction royalty for your composition being reproduced in the streaming service. The MLC collects this under the blanket license.

So from ONE STREAM, you should receive THREE payments:
1. Master royalty (via distributor) — arrives 2-3 months after the stream
2. Performance royalty (via PRO) — arrives 6-9 months after the stream (PROs are notoriously slow)
3. Mechanical royalty (via MLC) — arrives 3-6 months after the stream

Total Revenue Per Stream (approximate):
- Master: $0.003-$0.005 (from distributor)
- Performance: $0.001-$0.002 (from PRO)
- Mechanical: $0.0005-$0.001 (from MLC)
- Total: approximately $0.004-$0.008 per stream across all three flows

Common Missing Payments:
1. Not registered with MLC → Flow 3 is uncollected
2. No publisher entity registered with PRO → Publisher's Share of Flow 2 is uncollected
3. International streams without sub-publishing → Performance and mechanical royalties from foreign territories are uncollected
4. YouTube/ad-supported streams → Often lower rates and more complex collection

At 60K monthly listeners, ensuring all three flows are active is the difference between earning $200/month and $400/month from publishing alone.

What is The MLC and why does everyone say I need to register with them? Is it like ASCAP or BMI? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

The MLC (Mechanical Licensing Collective) is NOT the same as ASCAP or BMI, and registering with it is just as important. Here's the difference:

ASCAP/BMI/SESAC = Performance Rights Organizations (PROs): They collect PERFORMANCE royalties — money generated when your song is performed publicly (radio play, live concert venues, businesses playing music, streaming platforms). You register as a songwriter.

The MLC = Mechanical Licensing Collective: They collect MECHANICAL royalties from interactive streaming (Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon Music, YouTube Music) in the US. These are royalties owed to songwriters/publishers for the reproduction of compositions. DSPs pay a blanket license fee to The MLC, which then distributes to registered songwriters.

Why you MUST register: If you're not registered with The MLC, your streaming mechanical royalties go into an 'unmatched' pool. After 3 years, unmatched royalties are distributed to OTHER songwriters/publishers based on their market share. Translation: major publishers like Sony, Universal, and Warner collect YOUR unclaimed money.

How to register (free): Go to themlc.com. Create a songwriter account. Register all of your songs with complete metadata (title, ISRC, ISWC if available, co-writer info). Link your PRO membership (ASCAP/BMI IPI number).

Common misconception: 'My distributor handles this.' WRONG. Your distributor collects your MASTER recording royalties from streaming. The MLC collects your COMPOSITION/PUBLISHING mechanical royalties. These are two different income streams paid to two different entities.

How much money: Mechanical royalties from streaming are typically 20-30% of total streaming income for the composition side. If your music earns $10,000/year in master royalties from your distributor, you likely have $2,000-$3,000 in mechanical royalties owed through The MLC.

Quick registration stack for a self-published songwriter: (1) PRO (ASCAP or BMI) — for performance royalties. (2) The MLC — for US streaming mechanical royalties. (3) SoundExchange — for non-interactive streaming/radio royalties. (4) Your distributor — for master recording royalties. All four are separate. All four are necessary. Most are free to register.

A label offered me a deal and the contract mentions a 'controlled composition clause.' What is this and should I be worried? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Dante","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":200000,"goal":"label-deal"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Dante, the controlled composition clause is one of the most important — and most artist-unfriendly — terms in a record deal. Here is what you need to know.

What Is a Controlled Composition Clause?
A controlled composition clause applies to songs that are 'controlled' by you — meaning songs you wrote or co-wrote. It allows the record label to pay you LESS than the statutory mechanical rate for these compositions.

The Standard Statutory Rate: As of 2026, the statutory mechanical rate for a song under 5 minutes is approximately $0.12 per unit. This is what labels are REQUIRED by law to pay for songs written by OUTSIDE songwriters.

What the Controlled Composition Clause Does: It reduces the rate for YOUR songs — typically to 75% of the statutory rate (approximately $0.09 per unit). Additionally, it often caps the TOTAL mechanicals the label pays per album (e.g., 10 songs at 75% of statutory, regardless of how many songs are actually on the album).

Impact Example:
- 12-song album with no controlled composition clause: $0.12 x 12 = $1.44 per unit
- 12-song album WITH controlled composition clause (capped at 10 x 75%): $0.09 x 10 = $0.90 per unit
- Difference: $0.54 per unit — at 500,000 physical/download units, that is $270,000 in lost mechanicals

Why Labels Use It: Labels argue it reduces their cost structure. In the streaming era, controlled composition clauses are LESS impactful because streaming mechanical rates work differently (pool-based via the MLC). However, they still affect: physical sales (vinyl, CD), permanent downloads (iTunes), and the label's obligation under certain deal structures.

Negotiation Strategy:
1. Best outcome: Eliminate the controlled composition clause entirely (possible with leverage)
2. Compromise: Accept 75% rate but remove the per-album cap
3. Minimum: Ensure the clause does NOT apply to streaming mechanicals
4. Accept: If the advance is significant enough to offset the lost mechanicals

I got invited to a songwriting camp. There will be 20 writers I've never met. How do I protect my publishing and make sure I get credit? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Songwriting camps are amazing opportunities — they've produced massive hits. But they're also where publishing disputes are born if you don't protect yourself. Here's the protocol:

Before the camp: (1) Understand the camp's default split policy. Many camps have a 'room split' rule: everyone in the room when a song is written gets equal credit, regardless of contribution. A song written by 4 people = 25% each. Ask the camp organizer for their policy in writing. (2) Know your PRO info cold: your PRO (ASCAP/BMI), your IPI/CAE number, your legal name, and your publisher entity name (even if it's just you). (3) Bring pre-existing ideas/demos if you want, but know that once you bring something INTO a co-write, others may claim contribution to the final version.

During the session: (1) At the start of each session, agree verbally on the split arrangement: equal shares, or contribution-based. (2) Document who's in the room. Take photos, note names. (3) If you bring a pre-existing melody or lyric into the room that becomes part of the song, STATE IT CLEARLY: 'I'm bringing this chorus melody I wrote last week — I'd like to maintain my original contribution in the split.' (4) Don't be the person who sits on the couch playing their phone and then claims 25% because they were 'in the room.'

After the session: (1) SIGN A SPLIT SHEET that day — not 'later,' not 'when we get a deal.' NOW. The camp should provide forms. If they don't, bring your own. (2) The split sheet must include: song title (even a working title), all writers' legal names, PRO affiliations, IPI numbers, percentage splits, publisher information, date and signatures. (3) Take a photo of the signed split sheet immediately.

Post-camp: Register the song with your PRO within 2 weeks. Register with The MLC. Share the split sheet with any other writers' publishers who request it.

Protecting your pre-existing work: If you bring a finished or near-finished demo that gets 'improved' at the camp, negotiate for a larger share (40-50%). Alternatively, license it to the co-writers rather than splitting publishing.

Politics: Be generous but not foolish. Equal splits build relationships. Nickel-and-diming co-writers over 5% differences will earn you a reputation as difficult. But don't give up credit you've earned — that compounds over your career.

I've heard there are 'black box' royalties sitting unclaimed. How does that work and how do I make sure I'm not losing money to it? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"indie"}}]

Black box royalties are real, significant, and disproportionately affect independent artists. Here's exactly what they are and how to stop losing to them.

**What black box royalties are:**
When a streaming platform or broadcaster generates a royalty payment but can't match it to a specific rights holder — because the metadata is incomplete, the songwriter isn't registered, or the publishing information is missing — that money goes into an 'unmatched' or 'black box' pool. After a holding period (typically 3-5 years depending on the society), the money gets redistributed based on market share. Translation: your unclaimed royalties end up going to Universal, Sony, and Warner because they have the largest market share.

**How much money we're talking about:** Estimates vary, but the MLC (Mechanical Licensing Collective) in the US alone held $424 million in unmatched mechanical royalties as of early 2025. Globally, the number is likely $2-3 billion+ across all collection societies.

**How to make sure you're not losing to it:**
1. **Register with the MLC (themlc.com)** — free. They collect mechanical royalties from interactive streaming in the US. If you self-published and never registered, your mechanicals are in their black box right now.
2. **Register with your PRO as BOTH a writer AND publisher** — if you only registered as a writer, you're missing the publisher's share of performance royalties (that's 50% of your performance income).
3. **Get an IPI number** — this is your international publishing identifier. ASCAP/BMI assign one when you register. Make sure every song you distribute includes your IPI in the publishing metadata.
4. **Complete every metadata field** when distributing: songwriter legal name (consistent spelling), publisher name, IPI number, ISWC (if available). Every blank field is another chance for your royalty to go unmatched.
5. **Use a service like Songtrust or PubInTech** to register your catalog across 40+ international collection societies — many countries have their own pools of unmatched royalties.

The system is designed such that the least organized rights holders lose the most money. As an indie, that's you — unless you're meticulous about registration. Be meticulous.

I want to sample a hook from a 90s hip-hop classic. How much does it cost to clear a sample and what's the process on the publishing side? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Ace","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":120000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Ace, sample clearance involves two separate negotiations — one for the master recording and one for the composition (publishing). I will focus on the publishing side.

Publishing Sample Clearance:
When you sample a song, you are using the underlying composition (melody, lyrics, arrangement). The publisher of the original song must grant permission.

Typical Publishing Clearance Costs:
- Minor sample (short, non-recognizable): 10-15% of your song's publishing + possible upfront fee ($1,000-$5,000)
- Recognizable sample (clear hook or melody): 25-50% of your song's publishing + upfront fee ($2,500-$15,000)
- Iconic sample (the sample IS the song): 50-85% of your publishing + upfront fee ($10,000-$50,000+)

For a 90s hip-hop classic with a recognizable hook, expect: 25-50% of your publishing rights assigned to the original songwriters, plus a possible upfront advance of $3,000-$15,000.

The Process:
1. Identify the publisher: Search PRO databases (ASCAP ACE, BMI Repertoire) for the original song to find the publisher and songwriter(s).
2. Contact the publisher: Send a sample clearance request including your song (demo), the specific sample used, how prominently it features, and your proposed deal terms.
3. Negotiate: The publisher will counter with their terms. Key negotiation points are the percentage of publishing and any upfront fee.
4. Written agreement: Get a signed clearance agreement BEFORE releasing your song.
5. Registration: Register your song with the original songwriters listed as co-writers per the agreed split.

Timeline: 2 weeks to 6+ months. Some publishers are notoriously slow. Start early.

Alternatives If Clearance Is Too Expensive:
1. Interpolation: Re-perform the musical elements yourself. You only need to clear publishing (no master), and the publishing costs are typically lower.
2. Replay + small modification: Re-record the sample and alter it slightly (different key, tempo, or instrumentation). This may reduce the required publishing share.
3. Tracklib: A sample clearance platform with pre-negotiated rates for thousands of songs. Processing: often under 48 hours.

Critical: NEVER release a song with an uncleared sample. The consequences (takedown, lawsuit, statutory damages) far outweigh the clearance costs.

I used AI to help me write some melodies for my new album. Can I still register these songs and collect publishing royalties? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Zion","genre":"Electronic/Ambient","monthlyListeners":45000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Zion, this is a critical 2026 publishing issue. The short answer: yes, with important nuances. Here is the current legal framework.

The Core Rule: Human Authorship Required
The US Copyright Office requires human authorship for copyright registration. Works created entirely by AI cannot be copyrighted. However, works where AI is used as a TOOL with substantial human creative input CAN be registered.

The Spectrum of AI Use:

1. Fully Registrable (AI as Tool):
- You used AI to generate 10 melody options, then selected one, modified it, added lyrics, arranged it, and produced it. Your creative decisions are the copyrightable authorship.
- You used AI for harmony suggestions but wrote the melody and lyrics yourself.
- AI generated a chord progression, you built an entirely new composition on top of it.

2. Partially Registrable:
- You wrote lyrics and chorus melody, AI generated the verse melody. You can register the human-created elements but must disclose AI contributions.
- In registration, you would note: 'Music: partially generated with AI assistance, with substantial human selection, arrangement, and modification.'

3. Not Registrable:
- You typed a prompt like 'write me an ambient electronic song in C minor' and the AI produced the entire composition with no further human creative input.

Copyright Office Guidance:
In their 2023-2024 rulemaking and the ongoing 2025-2026 guidance, the Copyright Office stated: Authors must disclose AI-generated content in their application. The registration will cover only the human-authored portions. Failure to disclose AI involvement can result in cancellation of the registration.

Publishing Royalty Impact:
If your songs are registrable (AI-assisted, not AI-generated), you can register them with your PRO and the MLC and collect royalties normally. The key is to be transparent about the AI involvement and ensure your human creative contribution is substantial and documented.

Practical Advice:
1. Keep DAW project files showing your creative process
2. Document which elements were AI-generated vs. human-created
3. Make substantial creative modifications to any AI-generated elements
4. When registering, disclose AI assistance honestly
5. Your production, arrangement, mixing, and performance choices all count as human authorship

At your level, I recommend registering all works where you exercised meaningful creative control. The publishing royalties you would collect far outweigh the risk of overly cautious non-registration.

Neighboring rights are royalties paid to PERFORMERS and RECORDING OWNERS when their recorded music is played publicly. They're separate from and IN ADDITION TO your publishing/composition royalties.

**The Royalty Landscape:**

| Royalty Type | Who Gets Paid | Who Collects | Source |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|--------|
| Performance (composition) | Songwriter | PRO (ASCAP/BMI) | Radio, TV, venues |
| Mechanical (composition) | Songwriter | MLC / Publisher | Streaming, physical sales |
| **Neighboring Rights (recording)** | **Performer + Master Owner** | **SoundExchange / CMO** | **Digital radio, public performance** |
| Master royalties | Master owner | Distributor | Streaming, downloads |

**SoundExchange (US):**
- Collects digital performance royalties from Pandora, SiriusXM, and non-interactive streaming
- Splits: 45% featured artist, 5% session musicians (AFM/SAG-AFTRA fund), 50% master owner
- As an independent artist who owns your masters: you receive 95% (45% + 50%)
- **Free to register** — sign up at soundexchange.com
- Many independent artists have UNCOLLECTED royalties sitting there right now

**International Neighboring Rights:**
- Outside the US, neighboring rights also apply to terrestrial radio and public venues
- CMOs (Collective Management Organizations) collect in each territory:
  - UK: PPL
  - Germany: GVL
  - France: SCPP/SPPF
  - Japan: CPRA
- To collect internationally, register with a neighboring rights administrator:
  - Kobalt Neighboring Rights
  - Downtown Music
  - Songtrust

2. Register ALL recordings with ISRCs
3. Consider a neighboring rights administrator for international collection
4. Check if you have uncollected royalties — SoundExchange has a search tool

If I sign a record deal, does the label get my publishing too? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"label-deal","stage":"developing","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

This is one of the MOST important distinctions in the music business:

**Masters ≠ Publishing. They are SEPARATE rights.**

| Right | What It Covers | What a Label Wants |
|-------|---------------|--------------------|
| Master rights | The specific RECORDING | Almost always — this is a record deal |
| Publishing rights | The COMPOSITION (lyrics + melody) | Sometimes — especially in 360 deals |

**The Key Negotiation:**
A record deal grants the label rights to your RECORDINGS (masters). Your publishing (compositions) should be a SEPARATE negotiation.

**Label Publishing Tactics to Watch For:**
- **360 deal clause:** Some 360 deals include a publishing participation (10-25% of publishing income)
- **Affiliated publisher:** The label may own a publishing company and pressure you to sign with them
- **Co-publishing clause buried in the record contract:** Read EVERY page

**Your Strategy:**
1. **Keep publishing separate from the record deal.** Negotiate them independently.
2. **If the label also wants publishing, it should be a separate agreement** with separate advances and separate terms.
3. **Writer share is ALWAYS yours** — legally, the writer share (50%) cannot be taken by anyone.
4. **Focus on retaining the publisher share (50%)** — this is what's negotiable.

**The Independent Advantage:**
As an independent artist, you own 100% of your publishing by default. This is a major asset — some artists' publishing catalogs are worth millions. Don't give this away in a record deal without significant compensation.

Route to Legal before any publishing negotiation.

Someone told me I should put some music in a production music library for passive income. What are these and how do they work? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Hart","genre":"Cinematic/Instrumental","monthlyListeners":30000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Hart, production music libraries are an excellent passive income source, especially for cinematic/instrumental music. Here is how they work.

What Is a Production Music Library?
A curated collection of pre-licensed music used by content creators, TV shows, filmmakers, advertisers, podcasters, and YouTubers. Instead of commissioning original music, they browse the library and license tracks instantly.

How You Earn:
1. Sync/License Fees: A one-time or subscription-based fee paid by the content creator to use your music. Revenue split: typically 50/50 between you and the library.
2. Backend Performance Royalties: When your music airs on TV, radio, or streaming, you earn PRO royalties. This is where the real money is — a single TV placement can generate $200-$5,000+ in backend royalties per quarter.

Types of Libraries:

1. Exclusive Libraries (Higher Tier):
- They exclusively control the licensing rights to your submitted tracks
- You cannot license these same tracks anywhere else
- Higher placement quality: TV networks, major films, national commercials
- Examples: Extreme Music, APM, FirstCom, ScoreKeepers
- More curated, harder to get accepted

2. Non-Exclusive Libraries (Lower Barrier):
- You retain ownership and can submit the same tracks to multiple libraries
- Lower per-placement fees but more flexibility
- Examples: Pond5, AudioJungle, Artlist, Musicbed (partially)
- Easier to get accepted

3. Hybrid (Curated Non-Exclusive):
- Examples: Musicbed, Songtradr
- Curated quality but non-exclusive terms
- Growing category in 2026

Best Practices for Library Music:
1. Write music that serves a PURPOSE: tension, joy, sadness, energy, corporate inspiration
2. Create variations: 30-second, 60-second, and full-length versions of each track
3. Provide stems (drums only, no drums, no vocals, etc.)
4. Metadata is KING: tag extensively with mood, tempo, genre, instrumentation, use case
5. Volume matters: the more tracks you have in libraries, the more likely you get placed

Publishing Considerations:
- Exclusive libraries may require you to assign a percentage of publishing
- Non-exclusive libraries typically do NOT take publishing
- ALWAYS register your tracks with your PRO before submitting to any library
- Specify in the agreement that you retain Writer's Share regardless

A publisher is offering me a co-publishing deal at 75/25 in my favor. Is that good? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"hybrid","stage":"developing"}}]

75/25 is a fair-to-good headline split for a co-publishing deal — better than the 50/50 that used to be standard. But the 75/25 is the number they lead with because it sounds favorable. The real deal is in everything else.

Here's what 75/25 actually means: The publisher takes 25% of your copyright PERMANENTLY. Not for the term of the deal — forever. When the deal ends, they continue collecting their 25% of every dollar your catalog earns for the rest of your life, and potentially beyond. On a catalog that ends up earning $500k over its lifetime, that's $125k of your money that goes to them in perpetuity for a deal you signed early in your career.

Evaluate the whole deal on these terms:

1. **Admin fee** — Most co-pub deals charge 10-15% off the top of gross income before the 75/25 split. So you're not getting 75% of everything — you're getting 75% of 85-90%. Know this number.

2. **Advance** — How much, and is it recoupable? At what percentage? On gross or net collections?

3. **Term and catalog scope** — How long, and does it cover only songs written during the deal or your entire existing catalog? Existing catalog is a major concession.

4. **Reversion rights** — If the publisher isn't actively pitching your catalog (sync, co-writes, placements), you should be able to reclaim administration rights after a set period of inactivity. Without this, they can sit on your catalog and collect passively while doing nothing.

5. **At-source vs. receipts** — At-source means they collect in each territory and pass you your percentage of what was collected there. Receipts basis means they deduct sub-publishing fees before your percentage. At-source is always better for you.

6. **Audit rights** — Annual right to audit their accounting. Non-negotiable.

The 75/25 split isn't the question. The question is: what are you permanently giving up, and what are they actually going to do with your catalog?

I want to record a new version of a song from the 1920s. Is it public domain? Do I still need to clear publishing? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Cole","genre":"Jazz/Neo-Soul","monthlyListeners":65000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Cole, excellent question — public domain in music has important nuances. Here is the complete guide.

When Does Music Enter the Public Domain?
In the US, the rules depend on when the work was published:
- Published before 1928: Public domain as of 2024. No copyright protection.
- Published 1928-1977: Protected for 95 years from publication date (some may have had expired copyrights that were renewed or not)
- Published 1978 or later: Life of the author + 70 years

For a song from the 1920s: If published before 1928, the COMPOSITION (melody and lyrics) is in the public domain. You can freely record it, arrange it, and release it without clearing publishing rights.

CRITICAL Distinction — Composition vs. Recording:
- The COMPOSITION may be public domain
- Any SPECIFIC RECORDING of that composition is still protected if recorded after 1928
- You CANNOT sample a 1930 recording of a 1920s song — the recording has its own copyright
- You CAN record your OWN new version of the public domain composition

Arrangement Copyright:
If you create a substantially new arrangement of a public domain composition (new harmonies, new rhythmic treatment, bridge, etc.), your arrangement IS copyrightable as a derivative work. However: the underlying melody and lyrics remain public domain and anyone else can ALSO create their own arrangement.

Publishing Registration:
When you record a public domain song:
1. Register YOUR arrangement as a derivative work (if substantially different)
2. Credit: 'Written by [Original Composer] / Arranged by [Your Name]'
3. You can collect publishing royalties on the ARRANGEMENT portion
4. Some PROs allow you to register the arrangement with a note that the underlying work is PD

Gotchas:
- Some 'public domain' songs have had their copyrights restored (GATT/trips restoration for foreign works)
- Translations may be copyrighted even if the original language version is PD

What is a PRO and do I actually need to join one? I've been making music for 2 years and haven't signed up for anything. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"r&b"}}]

Yes — stop what you're doing and register today. Here's why it matters.

A PRO (Performing Rights Organization) is the entity that collects **performance royalties** on your behalf whenever your songs are played publicly — on radio, in a store, on Spotify, on TV, at a venue that pays a blanket license. In the US, the main PROs are ASCAP, BMI, and SESAC.

When you upload to Spotify, Spotify pays two parties: the owner of the master recording (you, via your distributor) AND the owner of the publishing (the songwriter). The publishing payment goes to your PRO. If you're not registered, that money sits in a pool that eventually gets distributed to other writers or returned. You get nothing.

**Two years unregistered = two years of uncollected royalties. That money is gone.**

How to fix it right now:
1. Go to ascap.com or bmi.com and sign up as both a writer AND a publisher (yes, both — one is how you credit your written songs, the other is how you collect the publisher's share too)
2. Registration is free for BMI; ASCAP has a one-time $50 fee
3. Register every song you've released — the title, the co-writers, the percentage splits
4. Going forward, register every new song BEFORE you release it

One more thing: your PRO collects performance royalties. A separate system called mechanical royalties (for streams and downloads) is collected through the MLC (Mechanical Licensing Collective) in the US — register there too at themlc.com. Also free.

I've been invited to a songwriting camp. What should I know about publishing splits and rights before I go? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Tay","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":110000,"goal":"label-deal"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Tay, songwriting camps are career-accelerating opportunities but you need to go in prepared on the publishing side. Here is everything you need to know.

What Is a Songwriting Camp?
A multi-day event where songwriters, producers, and topliner are grouped into sessions to co-write songs. Labels, publishers, or organizations host them to generate material for specific artists, projects, or catalogs. Camps range from intimate (10 writers, 3 days) to massive (50+ writers, a full week).

Publishing Splits at Camps:

1. Equal Split (Most Common): Everything created in a session is split equally among all writers in the room. 3 writers = 33.3% each, 4 writers = 25% each. This is the industry standard and avoids disputes.

2. Producer + Writer Split: If a producer provides the beat/track, they typically get a separate producer share. Common: producer gets 50% of publishing, remaining 50% split equally among writers/topliners.

3. Pre-Agreed Custom Splits: Some camps have pre-negotiated split structures. The camp organizer may specify the split methodology upfront.

Critical Pre-Camp Preparation:

1. Split Sheets: Bring blank split sheet templates. After EVERY session, get all participants to sign a split sheet documenting: song title (even if working title), date, all writer legal names, IPI numbers, PRO affiliations, agreed percentages, publisher information.

2. Do This IMMEDIATELY After the Session: Do NOT wait until after the camp. Memories fade, people leave, disputes arise months later. Sign the split sheet before anyone leaves the room.

3. IPI Number: Have your IPI number ready. Other writers will need it for their registrations.

4. Publishing Status: Know your publishing situation. If you have a publisher, they may have approval rights over co-writes. Check your publishing agreement for any restrictions.

5. Holds: Songs written at camps are often put on 'hold' for specific artists. A hold means the song is reserved and cannot be pitched to other artists. Holds are NOT legally binding unless contracted, but breaking a hold is considered professionally toxic.

Post-Camp:
- Register all songs you co-wrote with your PRO within 30 days
- Register with the MLC
- Follow up with co-writers to ensure their registrations match yours

How does being a songwriter help me get sync placements? Is it different from being the recording artist? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Being a songwriter is actually your BIGGEST advantage in sync licensing. Here's why:

The dual license requirement: every sync placement requires TWO licenses: (1) Sync license — permission to use the COMPOSITION (the song itself: melody, lyrics, arrangement). This is controlled by the SONGWRITER/PUBLISHER. (2) Master use license — permission to use the specific RECORDING. This is controlled by the LABEL/RECORDING OWNER. For major label artists, clearing both licenses requires negotiating with two separate entities (publisher + label). This can take weeks or months. For major label artists, it often kills deals — the timeline is too slow.

The indie songwriter advantage — 'One-Stop Licensing': If you WROTE the song AND own the master recording, you control BOTH licenses. You are a 'one-stop shop.' A music supervisor can clear your song with ONE email and ONE payment. This is ENORMOUSLY attractive to supervisors who are often working under tight deadlines (especially in advertising where they might need music cleared in 24-48 hours). Many music supervisors actively prefer working with independent artists specifically because of one-stop convenience.

How to position your songwriting catalog for sync: (1) Create a sync-ready catalog sheet: list every song with: title, genre, mood, tempo (BPM), lyrical themes, and whether it's one-stop clearable. (2) Record instrumentals: many sync placements use instrumentals only. Having instrumental versions of every song doubles your placement opportunities. (3) Create stems: separate your tracks into stems (drums, bass, vocals, melodic instruments). Supervisors love stems for custom edits and transitions. (4) Register with sync agents and music libraries: Musicbed, Songtradr, Artlist, Marmoset, Music Vine. These act as intermediaries between you and supervisors. (5) Pitch directly: find music supervisors on LinkedIn, the Guild of Music Supervisors directory, and at sync conferences (Sync Summit, SXSW sync panels).

Publishing deal impact on sync: If you have a publishing admin (like Songtrust): they handle licensing paperwork but you retain control. They may take 10-15% commission. If you have a co-publishing deal: your publisher actively pitches your songs for sync placements (a major benefit). They take 25-50% of sync income. If you're self-published: you keep 100% but must handle all licensing administration yourself.

Sync income splits: the sync fee is typically split 50/50 between the master owner and the composition owner. If you own both: you keep 100% of the entire sync fee.

I cleared a sample for my new track and the publisher wants 50% of my publishing. Is that standard? What does this mean for my money long-term? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

50% of your publishing for a sample clearance is common but not necessarily fair — and it depends heavily on how the sample is used. Let me break down the landscape:

What's typical: For a recognizable, prominent sample (the hook or main melody): 25-50% of publishing. For a minor, background sample (a drum loop or short texture): 5-15% of publishing. For an interpolation (you replayed it, not direct sample): 10-25% of publishing.

What 50% publishing actually costs you: Let's say your track earns $10,000 in total publishing royalties over its lifetime. At 50%, you're giving up $5,000. If you wrote the entire song otherwise, that $5,000 is going to the sampled songwriter/publisher for a 4-second loop you could potentially have replayed or replaced.

Negotiation strategies: (1) Counter-offer at 15-25% for non-prominent samples. Publishers often start high expecting negotiation. (2) Offer a flat fee instead of a percentage — 'I'll pay $2,000 upfront for clearance, no ongoing royalty share.' Some publishers prefer guaranteed money. (3) Propose a cap — '25% of publishing up to $10,000, then it reverts to 10%.' (4) If the sample is from a less commercially valuable catalog, you have more leverage. (5) Have your entertainment attorney negotiate — they know the market rates.

Long-term impact: Publishing royalties are potentially lifelong income (life of copyright = your life + 70 years). Giving up 50% of a hit song's publishing is giving up income for generations. The song 'Bitter Sweet Symphony' by The Verve famously lost 100% of publishing to The Rolling Stones for a sample clearance. Think carefully.

Alternative: Can you replay the sample with session musicians and interpolate instead? This often allows you to negotiate a much lower publishing share (10-15%) since you're not using the actual master recording.

I keep hearing I should start my own publishing company. How do I do that and is it actually worth it? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Setting up your own publishing entity is one of the smartest moves for a self-released songwriter. Here's why and how:

Why you need your own publisher: When you register songs with your PRO (ASCAP or BMI), each song has TWO shares: the writer's share (50%) — paid directly to you as the songwriter. The publisher's share (50%) — paid to your publisher. If you DON'T have a publishing entity registered with your PRO, the publisher's share may go uncollected or pooled. By creating your own publishing company and affiliating it with your PRO, you collect BOTH shares — 100% of performance royalties.

Step-by-step setup: (1) Choose a publishing company name. It must be UNIQUE — your PRO will check it against existing publishers. Prepare 3-5 options in case your first choice is taken. The name does NOT need to match your artist name. Example: you're 'DJ Example' — your publishing company could be 'Night Sky Music Publishing.' (2) Form a business entity (recommended but not required). LLC is the most common choice for indie publishers. Cost: $50-$500 depending on your state. This provides liability protection and simplifies taxes. (3) Affiliate your publishing company with your PRO. ASCAP: online application at ascap.com. One-time fee: $50. BMI: online application at bmi.com. No fee for publishers. Your WRITER account and your PUBLISHER account must be with the SAME PRO. (4) Register all your songs under both your writer account AND your publisher account. Each song registration should list: you as the writer (writer's share) and your publishing entity as the publisher (publisher's share). Percentages: writer 50%, publisher 50%.

What your publishing entity can do: Collect the publisher's share of performance royalties (ASCAP/BMI). Issue mechanical licenses and sync licenses on behalf of your catalog. Register with The MLC as a publisher to collect mechanical royalties. Register with international societies via an admin service (Songtrust, etc.). License your music for sync placements.

Do you need an admin service on top of this? For US performance royalties: NO — your PRO + publisher affiliation handles this. For global collection: YES — use an admin service like Songtrust ($100/year) to register internationally. For mechanical royalties: register directly with The MLC (free) under your publisher entity. For sync licensing: you can self-administer or use a sync agent.

Cost summary: PRO publisher affiliation: $0-$50 one-time. LLC formation: $50-$500 one-time. Admin service (optional): $50-$100/year. Total upfront: $50-$550. This is a one-time investment that ensures you collect 100% of your publishing income for life.
